essence: Anthropic-sponsored Chiang Mai workshop (Ian as Thailand ambassador, Nick hosting) clarified the Max-subscription policy — Agents SDK / signature-spoofing use is fine for local personal work but banned for public revenue apps, with Sonnet 4.6 (~Opus 4.5 quality at 1/5 cost) as the sanctioned production path — demoed Claude desktop's new in-app dev-server previews and Cowork's productivity-plugin personal agent (working memory, deep memory, glossary, dossiers), and reached consensus that most third-party skills are low-quality or dangerous (crypto-wallet attack vectors), so audit or rebuild them via skill-creator and use pointer/lazy-loading skills instead of 200-skill monorepos.

actions.text: Set up David's Friday event as a Luma event for easier distribution

essence: Before the workshop David demoed POEM (Penny/Alex/Oscar agents; DSL and YAML + JSON schemas + Handlebars prompts compiled to JavaScript workflows with parallel predicates, classifiers, and observation agents on a shared data bus) to a developer rolling his own DAG control plane in OpenCode, advising compose-don't-rebuild (Pydantic/LangChain/LangGraph/Vercel AI SDK), get the workflow layer right before picking a UI, and use streamed events for human-in-the-loop gates.

actions.text: Developer to message David to set up a lunch meeting comparing workflow-orchestration approaches

essence: Video excerpt introducing Kintsugi — Sonar's free, experimental, macOS-only agentic development environment layered on top of Claude Code (not replacing it) that visualizes parallel agent sessions, lets you inspect/comment on plans, presents output as PR-style diffs instead of raw logs, and applies review types including security review.

David flags a risk in storing the second brain in GitHub while also using Obsidian Sync: files moving between machines outside standard Git mechanisms can trigger unnecessary commits and merge conflicts.

loop.label: Obsidian Sync vs Git conflict risk

essence: After watching Cole Medin's second-brain workshop (transcript captured), David directs that Slack should be added as a messaging/integration channel to both the Ansible setup and potentially the agentic OS.

actions.text: Add Slack integration to Ansible and potentially the agentic OS

essence: Cole Medin's Dynamous workshop walked through his personal Claude Code + Agent SDK second brain — soul.md/user.md/memory.md/daily-logs memory with session-start and pre-compact hooks, hybrid RAG memory search, a query.py router keeping API keys out of LLM context with read-only integrations (Gmail/Calendar/Asana/Slack/Circle), a 30-min heartbeat on a locked-down Ubuntu VPS (Opus pass + Haiku condenser, ~$0.05/run on subscription), Atomic-Habits pillar tracking, and the rule PydanticAI for production agents vs Agent SDK for personal ones — a smaller, auditable alternative to OpenClaw he mined for ideas by pointing Claude at its cloned repo.

Café conversation: David is stretched across two-going-on-three clients plus YouTube while funding Joy's expansion — two-storey nail spa plus a new juice shop built onto the side (two Myanmar staff, targeting 50-60k THB/month to clear her 36%-interest COVID-era debt, blender still needed, opening within days) — his six-month visa exit lands March 24 (Vietnam or Malaysia, flights not booked), and a close friend detailed his progressive lung disease prognosis and end-of-life planning.

actions.text: Check flights to Vietnam or Malaysia for the March 24 visa exit
Buy a blender so the juice shop can open

essence: Inaugural Agents in the Wild meetup in Chiang Mai: Nick presented his pre-OpenClaw personal agent 'Ghost' and the agent-first paradigm (reconstruct reality away from algorithmic feeds, offload every manual task, control the autonomy knob, use local community against AI isolation); Ian's lightning talk covered Samantha's 9-component architecture (harness/orchestrator/sub-agents/skills/brain/scheduling/observability/self-extension, Kybernesis sleep-time memory) ahead of Samantha autonomously running the March 7 event logistics; open discussion covered A2A protocols (TLS/mTLS, discovery registries, HTTP gateway brokers), sandboxing, and keeping outbound comms human-in-the-loop.

Pre-meetup demos: a peer showed his self-hosted image-generation stack (open-source models on a GPU box exposed via Tailscale private APIs, reusable prompt presets, queueing, planned free web hosting with SEO blog content and animation upsells), and David demoed his multi-agent workflow framework (prompt engineer / architect / orchestrator agents, Handlebars + JSON schemas, YAML compiled to JavaScript with parallel steps) via the incident-analysis workflow — while conceding it is a learning project unlikely to be robust enough for real-world deployment.

Dent value-canvas workshop check-in (David still on ICP stress test): facilitator pushed rough-draft-over-perfection, banned 'help' and 'empower' from pitches (pointing to his YouTube video with a rephrasing formula rather than word swaps), confirmed the portal cannot yet re-edit previous value canvases (exported PDFs are the only workaround, fix in progress), and ran another 15-minute Pomodoro sprint toward the prize section.

actions.text: Finish the value canvas through pain, deep fear, and prize sections

essence: Dent Chaos Group 2.5-hour value-canvas workshop kickoff: done vs not-done split by the (buggy) blue Export PDF button, rationale that people only pay for value they can see and the canvas seeds all marketing messaging, guidance to stay 'Batman' over the AI builder, focus the ICP on the payer not the end user, and Pomodoro sprints in a silent chat-only workspace with staff support.
